skill.name: lark-workflow-standup-report
skill.description: Generates a daily or weekly Lark standup summary from calendar events and unfinished tasks using lark-cli.
skill.summary: Summarize Lark calendar events, unfinished tasks, conflicts, and free time for a selected date.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, weak cryptography, and entropy patterns in SKILL.md. Review confirms the command patterns are documented lark-cli invocations for an authenticated calendar and task workflow, while the filesystem, weak cryptography, and entropy hits are false positives from Markdown links and Chinese text. The remaining risk is medium because the skill directs execution of external CLI commands that retrieve sensitive calendar and task data.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Lark CLI Access to Sensitive Work Data
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs use of lark-cli auth login and lark-cli calendar and task commands. This is legitimate for the workflow, but it can expose calendar events, task titles, due dates, RSVP status, and availability data to the assistant session.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and required scopes are explicit in SKILL.md. The risk is data exposure from authenticated calendar and task retrieval, not command injection.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Flagged as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The ../lark-shared, ../lark-calendar, and ../lark-task references are Markdown links to sibling skill documentation. No evidence found that these paths are used for file reads, writes, or arbitrary filesystem access by executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path traversal pattern is present, but the context is Markdown documentation. There is no script or code path that consumes these paths.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Entropy Findings Are Scanner False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hit points to the front matter description, and the entropy hit points to the file start. No cryptographic API, encoded payload, binary content, or obfuscated script is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines contain YAML front matter and Chinese description text. There is no executable cryptographic or obfuscated content.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Commands in Workflow Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell examples for lark-cli authentication, calendar agenda retrieval, and task retrieval. These commands are expected for the workflow but should run only after user approval and with awareness of the requested Lark scopes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are directly documented and operate on authenticated Lark data. They are not malicious, but they are security-relevant external commands.

content.value_statement: Daily planning is slow when meetings and tasks live in separate Lark tools. This skill gathers agenda and task data, then produces a concise standup summary with conflicts and free time.
actual_capabilities: Runs lark-cli calendar agenda commands for today or an ISO 8601 date range.
Runs lark-cli task retrieval commands for unfinished tasks and due-date filters.
Combines meetings and tasks into a daily or weekly standup summary.
Converts event timestamps into readable local times using timezone data.
Detects overlapping accepted or tentative calendar events.
Sorts agenda items and tasks by start time or due date.
limitations: Requires lark-cli to be installed and authenticated for calendar and task scopes.
Only supports user identity workflows, not application identity workflows.
Date ranges must be converted to ISO 8601 values before running commands.
Large task lists may need filtering to avoid long assistant context.

use_cases.title: Daily standup preparation
use_cases.description: Review today’s Lark meetings, unfinished tasks, conflicts, and free time before a team standup.

use_cases.title: Manager schedule review
use_cases.description: Create a concise planning view for a busy day without checking calendar and task tools separately.

use_cases.title: Weekly workload scan
use_cases.description: Summarize the week’s meetings and open tasks to identify scheduling pressure and overdue work.

prompt_templates.title: Today’s summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize my Lark calendar and unfinished tasks for today. Include meetings, due tasks, conflicts, and free time.

prompt_templates.title: Tomorrow’s plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a standup report for tomorrow from my Lark calendar and tasks. Convert the date to ISO 8601 before using lark-cli.

prompt_templates.title: Filtered task review
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my meetings and only tasks due by the end of this week. Mark overdue tasks and keep older undated tasks collapsed.

prompt_templates.title: Conflict-aware weekly report
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a weekly Lark standup report. Detect meeting overlaps, exclude declined events from busy time, and summarize open task risk.

output_examples.output: A dated standup summary with a meeting table, unfinished task checklist, total meeting count, conflict notes, and estimated free slots.
A next-day agenda using ISO 8601 date filters, sorted meetings, due tasks, RSVP labels, and a short planning summary.

output_examples.input: Review this week and highlight conflicts.
output_examples.output: A weekly planning summary that groups meetings by time, flags overlapping events, lists due tasks, and separates older historical tasks.

best_practices: Confirm the target date and timezone before running lark-cli commands.
Use due-date filters for task summaries to reduce unnecessary data exposure.
Review requested calendar and task scopes before authorizing lark-cli.
anti_patterns: Do not run the authentication command without user consent.
Do not pass natural language dates directly to lark-cli date options.
Do not include every historical task when a focused summary is requested.

faq.question: What Lark data does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses calendar agenda data and unfinished task data available through authenticated lark-cli commands.

faq.question: Does it support natural language dates in lark-cli?
faq.answer: No. The assistant must convert dates such as tomorrow into ISO 8601 or Unix timestamp values first.

faq.question: Can it find meeting conflicts?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares sorted event times and flags overlapping events that are not declined.

faq.question: Can it summarize weekly plans?
faq.answer: Yes. It can use a date range and task due-date filter to create a weekly planning summary.

faq.question: What permissions are required?
faq.answer: The documented commands require calendar event read access and task read access for the current user.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive calendars?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill retrieves work calendar and task details, so users should limit dates and filters to the needed scope.
